dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 47 responding for convulsions/seizure.
Hi, I'm Jordan, an EMS provider. What brought you to call 911?
Patient was found on the floor of the dialysis clinic after a witnessed grand mal seizure and head injury from the fall.
Glasgow Coma Scale is 10; eyes are equal and reactive to light, but the patient is in altered mental status and non-verbal.
Pulse is 104, respiratory rate is 16, blood pressure is 138/84, SpO2 is 100% on room air, and EKG shows sinus tachycardia.
Blood glucose is 117 mg/dL on the initial assessment and cannot be retrieved in later readings.
A cervical collar was applied due to altered mental status and a head injury.
A pediatric cervical collar was used because the standard size was too large for the patient.
No DCAP-BTLS identified during the physical exam; head shows a 1 cm laceration with swelling on the left side.
Intravenous access established with a 20-gauge IV in the right arm.
4-lead ECG monitoring initiated. The patient remains in sinus tachycardia.
Oxygen given at 2 liters per minute via nasal cannula and ceased after an SpO2 of 98%.
Patient has End Stage Renal Disease and depends on dialysis. A full session was performed earlier; she receives treatment every Tuesday, Thursday, and Saturday.
History of mild asthma, managed with Albuterol as needed.
Patient has epilepsy with known seizure history; this was a witnessed grand mal seizure reported to last about one minute.
The patient has fetal alcohol syndrome and communicates with head-shaking yes/no; unable to respond to EMS initially.
Patient has latex allergy; latex-free equipment used during care.
Current medications include Heparin, Folic Acid, and Albuterol. Known Penicillin allergy noted.
No DCAP-BTLS noted on the head, chest, abdomen, or back during the physical exam.
Current vitals: pulse 103, blood pressure 139/83, SpO2 97%.
SpO2 slightly improved to 98%, pulse decreased to 101, blood pressure is 143/83.
Patient showed no signs of discomfort; score is 0.
Transporting to [COMPANY_NAME] due to altered mental status, head injury, and seizure protocol.
Patient moved onto the stretcher and secured with a 5-point harness.
Chest rising and falling equally, no accessory muscles used.
No DCAP-BTLS found on the chest, consistent with patent airway.
Abdomen soft, non-tender, and no acute distress.
No DCAP-BTLS found on abdominal exam.
Extremity pulses are strong and regular; no visible injuries or DCAP-BTLS.
No DCAP-BTLS on the back, flank, or buttocks.
Eyes open spontaneously but patient is not fully oriented.
No verbal response due to non-verbal baseline.
Patient displayed gross motor response but could not indicate yes/no.
Airway patent and without signs of obstruction.
Respirations at 16/min; regular and unlabored, no cyanosis reported.
Peripheral pulses strong and regular, no signs of poor perfusion.
Skin is warm and dry, consistent with baseline.
Patient had just completed dialysis 30 minutes prior.
The nurse reported the patient fell during a seizure, causing a head injury.
EKG shows ongoing sinus tachycardia as in initial reading.
Sinus tachycardia consistently noted in all EKG readings.
SpO2 ranges between 100% and 97% depending on assessment time.
IV access showed no abnormalities or complications.
Cervical collar remains in place without affecting breathing.
Altered mental status per normal for patient at baseline.
Communication too limited to assess short-term memory.
No signs of swallowing difficulties or neck rigidity noted.
No neck masses or lymph nodes palpable during assessment.
No recent injuries or new scars identified on body or head.
No fluid, blood, or obvious damage noted in ears or eyes.
No signs of blockage or bleeding from nasal passages.
Oral cavity free of swelling or lesions.
Equal breath sounds in all lobes, no abnormal sounds.
Heart sounds regular, no systolic or diastolic murmurs.
No pain or rigidity, gastrointestinal sounds present.
No reports of pelvic pain or bleeding from rectal exam.
Genital assessment skipped for patient privacy and non-verbal status.
Spine visually intact with no tenderness or injury signs.
No additional medications administered; no indication reported.
Administering protocol 3-15, 3-12, and 4-6 based on the findings.
Stretcher re-checked before ambulance loading for transport.
All devices and fluids secure for ED transfer upon arrival.
Known history includes fetal alcohol syndrome and epilepsy.
Voluntary movement noted in all limbs, no paralysis observed.
Glucose trending low on second and third checks; initial value at 117.
Pupil size equal and reaction to light consistent throughout.
No signs of fractures or misalignment in limb structures, no DCAP-BTLS.
Patient not consuming orally; receiving dialysis for hydration.
Patient harness securely fastened for ideal transport safety.
Monitoring via device every five minutes, no significant changes seen.
Stretcher transferred to ambulance using full team lift.
Ambulance en route, vitals being closely watched.
No fractures or structural misalignment in body parts.
Continuing to destination, making progress.
All latex and Penicillin alerts in place and materials free of both.
Masks and tape are non-latex as per safety guidelines.
Equipment in ambulance fully locked and secured for motions.
No new changes in vitals during the transport.
Alerting ED upon arrival to provide transition handover.
C-collar remained stable and breathing unimpeded.
EKG monitor displayed no new cardiac issues during transport.
Patient's skin continues to remain in normal appearance.
All protocols followed and completed appropriately during call.
ED nurse assumed control; EMS role complete at that point.
Patient handed over in good condition; protocols fully adhered to.
Patient had a witnessed seizure with fall-related head injury, on dialysis.
Full evaluations completed; no missing components in documentation.
Emergency department staff advised ahead of arrival.
All testing completed and documented correctly per protocols.
No significant findings changed the care or plans during evaluation.
Patient transferred to ED on stretcher without transport incident.
Verbal transfer confirmed with ED nurse at hospital entrance.
Manual notes made due to high call volume; unable to print PDR.
Transportation completed successfully; patient received in condition.
Call handled as per normal training; documentation pending post-debrief.
Returning to station; debriefing initiated on transport notes and protocols.
